key_points: Light travels in straight lines and follows predictable laws when it reflects or refracts
The law of reflection states that the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection
Concave mirrors can produce real, inverted images or virtual, magnified images depending on the position of the object
Convex mirrors always produce virtual, upright, and diminished images with a wide field of view
Convex lenses converge parallel light rays to a focal point, while concave lenses diverge light rays
Image formation by mirrors and lenses depends on the distance between the object and the optical device
examples: Concave mirrors used in headlights to produce strong beams of light
Convex mirrors used in security mirrors and vehicle rear-view mirrors
Convex lenses used in cameras, microscopes, and magnifying glasses
Concave lenses used in spectacles to correct short-sightedness
formulas: Law of reflection → Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection
Mirror image property → Distance of image from mirror = Distance of object from mirror
Refraction principle → Light bends when it passes between different media
exam_tips: Explain clearly how image formation changes with object position
Differentiate between real and virtual images in answers
Use diagrams when describing concave mirror or convex lens behavior
Remember practical applications of mirrors and lenses in instruments
